dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a sick person/shortness of breath at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Something just changed. His eyes rolled back, and he's not responding.
Thank you. The wife is with us and reports he became unresponsive and was initially experiencing shortness of breath. We're taking it from here.
Can you open your eyes for me, sir?
No response to voice or touch. Eyes remain closed.
Can you respond to a question for me, like your name?
No response to verbal or painful stimuli. Global GCS is three.
Airway appears patent and unobstructed at this point. Any history of airway issues?
No eye opening observed. Eyes are closed and not reactive.
Suction has been applied to clear secretions. Airway is now clear.
Checking breathing now. Slow, shallow respirations-10 per minute. Equal bilateral chest rise.
Respirations are 10 currently.
Pulse is weak but detectable. Heart rate is 65. BP is 91/55. Skin is pale, warm, and dry.
Pulse is 65 and regular.
Blood pressure is 91 over 55.
SpO2 is 97% using the finger sensor.
Capillary blood glucose is 387 mg/dL.
We are about to begin assisted ventilations.
We are assisting with BVM now. Let's monitor for effective ventilations.
Ma'am, did he have any symptoms before this occurred, such as cough, body aches or sweating?
Yes, he had a cough, body aches, and sweating for much of the morning. He was completely alert but not breathing normally.
Thank you. Any known medical history?
He has insulin-dependent diabetes and hypertension.
Any medications he is currently on?
Metformin, Microzide, metoprolol, and pravastatin, if I remember correctly.
No trauma or deformities found in the extremities or back during assessment.
Exiting to Protocol 2.7 for cardiac arrest.
Pulse is dropping to 0.
He is now apneic and has no pulse. Initiating CPR now.
Starting compressions with the manual CPR hand cycle-standard speed and depth.
Applying four-lead ECG monitoring now. Found PEA. Notifying the RFD and EMS1 for backup.
Establishing intraosseous access in the left humeral head now.
No pain response observed due to unresponsiveness.
First dose of Epinephrine, 1mg 1:10,000, administered via IO.
Initiating Autopulse for external compressions now.
SpO2 has dropped to 43%. Significant desaturation detected.
No pulse and still apneic.
CPR on Autopulse and BVM-assisted ventilations will be continued during intubation.
Intubating with 7.5 mm oral tube. Good breath sounds and end-tidal CO2 at 50.
RFD is arriving. On Autopulse with compressions and BVM. Preparing his transfer to the Reeves.
Transporting emergently to Chippenham Emergency Department.
Patient is now on the stretcher and fully secured for transport.
Second dose of Epinephrine, 1mg IO, administered successfully per protocol.
ED, incoming is a 62-year-old male in cardiac arrest with two IO Epinephrine doses administered.
ROSC in sinus rhythm. Autopulse is still in use to maintain chest compressions.
BP is now 99/38. No signs of deterioration.
Cardiovascular rhythm currently is PEA with return of sinus rhythm after ROSC.
Establishing IV line with normal saline under pressure bag support as per protocol.
Patient was transferred from EMS stretcher to trauma bed using the Reeves with Autopulse still attached.
BP at 99/38 during transport. Stable for now.
Trauma room two is notified and awaiting the patient's arrival.
SpO2 remains at 43% despite ongoing ventilations and Epinephrine.
Assisted respirations steady at 12 per minute.
History includes insulin-dependent diabetes and hypertension with known medications: metformin, glipizide, metoprolol, and pravastatin.
Glucose was 387 at initial assessment. Known hyperglycemia from diabetes history.
EKG shows return of spontaneous circulation with effective airway confirmed.
Receiving team, compressions are ongoing and patient is being monitored securely en route.
Autopulse remains active during transport with continuous monitoring.
All on board and ready for transport.
Breathing remains stable at 12 per minute with low oxygen saturation.
Close monitoring still required for full stabilization.
ED team has taken over at Chippenham and is in charge of care now.
ROSC maintained with a stable sinus rhythm during transport.
Autopulse is operational and compressions are effective per medical records.
EMS has completed all interventions and passed care to the trauma team.
Rechecking BP. Confirmed as 99/38 during transport.
PEA rhythm identified during initial monitoring. Transitioned to ROSC.
Oral endotracheal tube in place, size 7.5, confirmed with capnography. Position stable.
ETCO2 of 50 mmHg, verifies proper tube placement and ventilatory effort.
Endotracheal intubation performed en route for airway control. Capnography used to confirm.
Ongoing management as per cardiac arrest protocol, consistent with ground truth.
ECG shows sinus rhythm established, with continuous monitoring.
Pulse dropped from 65 to 0, and then improved with ROSC.
Ongoing oxygenation efforts continue with BVM and ET tube in place.
Regaining radial pulse with no peripheral pulses felt initially.
Blood pressure initially 91 over 55, then dropped after loss of pulse before ROSC.
EMT confirming BP at 99/38 again, per ePCR time stamp.
SpO2 previously at 97%, then decreased to 43% during apnea, and no improvement reported.
Preferred posture for airway adjustment is standard supine with head in neutral position.
All interventions followed protocol, no deviations observed.
Average blood pressure remained measured as 91-99 / 38-55 during assessment.
Patient remains under continuous monitoring, including ECG leads.
EKG leads and monitoring devices stabilized before patient transfer.
Autopulse used throughout the transport along with support monitoring.
Patient fully intubated with endotracheal tube confirmation by ETCO2.
Patient initial assessment showed chief symptoms of cardio-respiratory non-function.
No overwhelming new findings from intra-hospital assessment en route.
Patient aged 62 with known risks of diabetes and hypertension.
Physical weight of 120 kg posed logistical challenge but met.
Careful transfers executed from bedroom to stretcher with assistance from RFD.
Expected outcomes ongoing post-cardiac arrest, under ED care now.
Final check confirms patient safe, secure, and on BP support line.
Parameters are still within expected range for post-ROSC en route.
Trauma room two occupied, with full staff present for ongoing management.
Safe transport ensured, with uninterrupted medical monitoring system.
No reports of pain observed at any stage. Pain score is 0.
Initial rhythm was PEA. ROSC occurred during transport but rhythm remains unstable.
Normal saline IV is running under a pressure bag to maintain adequate flow.
